speaker: patient
text: Hi, Doctor. I’ve been feeling unusually tired lately, and my legs have been swollen for about a week.

speaker: doctor
text: Thank you for coming in. Based on your symptoms and lab work, we’ve confirmed a diagnosis of hypothyroidism, which likely started around early June.

speaker: patient
text: Yes, the swelling is mostly in my ankles and calves — it’s worse by evening, and pressing leaves a slight dent.

speaker: doctor
text: I just measured your blood pressure: systolic is 107 mmHg.

speaker: doctor
text: And diastolic is 102 mmHg — that’s quite elevated, especially the diastolic component.

speaker: patient
text: Really? That seems high. I’ve been checking at home sometimes — last week it was 138 over 86, but today before coming in, it read 142 over 94.

speaker: doctor
text: Given your persistent hypertension and the edema, I’m prescribing amlodipine 10 mg once daily, starting today.

speaker: patient
text: Okay, I’ll start that. My sister takes amlodipine too — she said it helped her swelling.

speaker: doctor
text: That’s consistent — calcium channel blockers like amlodipine can help with both BP and peripheral edema. Let’s also check your heart rate.

speaker: doctor
text: Your pulse is 53 bpm — that’s on the lower side, especially with hypothyroidism and now starting a calcium channel blocker.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve noticed I get lightheaded when standing up quickly — could that be related?

speaker: doctor
text: Possibly. Hypothyroidism slows heart rate, and amlodipine can add to that. We’ll monitor closely — let’s repeat thyroid function tests next week.

speaker: patient
text: Also, I’ve been taking levothyroxine 50 mcg since last month — but yesterday my home BP cuff showed 110 over 104, which matches your reading earlier.

speaker: doctor
text: That diastolic number — 104 — is concerning. It’s close to the 102 we saw just now, so that confirms persistent diastolic hypertension.

speaker: patient
text: Right, that’s the same reading as before — I remember your note said '102 mmHg' — that number really stood out to me.

speaker: doctor
text: Exactly — and given your bradycardia and borderline hypotension risk, I’m going to switch you from amlodipine to losartan 50 mg daily instead.

speaker: patient
text: Losartan? I tried that years ago — it made my potassium go up. Should I get that checked again?

speaker: doctor
text: Yes, absolutely. We’ll check renal function and potassium before starting, and again in one week. Also, continue levothyroxine — we may need to titrate it up once your TSH comes back.

speaker: patient
text: Got it. And just to clarify — that low heart rate of 53, is that why you’re changing the BP med?

speaker: doctor
text: Yes — combining amlodipine with your underlying bradycardia and hypothyroid state increases risk of symptomatic bradycardia or hypotension. Losartan is safer in this context.
